title: Hospital Delay in Meningitis Outbreak Alert
summary: A hospital in the UK waited two days before telling health officials about a meningitis outbreak. Meningitis is a serious illness that can spread quickly. The hospital, called Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother Hospital, had a patient with meningitis on Wednesday, but they did not report it until Friday. This delay meant that people who were close to the patient could not be warned right away. Experts say this was a mistake because hospitals must report suspected cases immediately, not wait for test results. Two people died, and four were in intensive care. The hospital admitted they should have acted sooner to protect others.

background_read: Meningitis is a dangerous infection that can cause swelling in the brain and spinal cord. It spreads through close contact, like sharing drinks or coughing. Symptoms include fever, headache, and a stiff neck. Hospitals must report suspected cases right away to health officials so they can warn others and stop the spread. In the UK, there are rules that require this immediate reporting to protect public health.
Article_Structure: WHO: The Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother Hospital and health officials
WHAT: A delay in reporting a meningitis outbreak
WHY: To protect people by warning them quickly and tracing contacts

perspectives.description: The hospital said they waited for a formal diagnosis before reporting, but admitted they missed an opportunity to act sooner.
